findings.justification: Three anomalies were flagged by Agent 1: an orphaned parent (PPID 2600 absent from pslist), a near-complete SYSTEM-level privilege token with SeTcbPrivilege, SeDebugPrivilege, and SeImpersonatePrivilege all enabled simultaneously, and a non-standard DLL (C:\WINDOWS\SYSTEM32\UpdateReboot.dll). The orphaned-parent signal is real but weak in isolation — a parent process exiting after spawning a child is normal Windows behaviour for service-hosting chains. The privilege set is extensive but is consistent with AggregatorHost.exe running legitimately as SYSTEM (the service does run under the SYSTEM account and inherits a full SYSTEM token). The decisive indicator would be the UpdateReboot.dll load, but the dlllist.txt grep block provided to this analyst shows only 3 of 28 matching lines — all three are benign System32 modules — and UpdateReboot.dll does not appear in any supplied evidence line; it cannot be cited or confirmed from the available input. No malfind sections, no network connections, and no suspicious command-line arguments were present in the evidence to provide additional corroboration. A definitive verdict requires the full dlllist output for PID 1708 to be reviewed.

evidence.content: 1708	AggregatorHost	7	SeTcbPrivilege	Present,Enabled,Default	Act as part of the operating system
1708	AggregatorHost	29	SeImpersonatePrivilege	Present,Enabled,Default	Impersonate a client after authentication

findings.justification: The command line unambiguously executes a randomly-named batch file (EMwLtc9FBmME.bat) from the user Temp directory under the 'kali' profile — a classic dropper or stager pattern. The parent PID 4240 is absent from the process list, indicating either a short-lived launcher that has already exited or deliberate PPID spoofing, both of which are consistent with attacker tradecraft. The privilege set is anomalous for a user-context cmd.exe: the token carries the full Windows privilege catalogue including SeTcbPrivilege ("Act as part of the operating system") and SeDebugPrivilege, which together allow arbitrary process memory access and token impersonation — far beyond what a legitimate interactive shell would hold. The child process tree (conhost.exe for I/O, PING.EXE one second later) matches a common "ping-based sleep" anti-sandbox delay pattern embedded in batch stagers.

evidence.content: 2380	cmd.exe	7	SeTcbPrivilege		Act as part of the operating system
2380	cmd.exe	29	SeImpersonatePrivilege		Impersonate a client after authentication

findings.justification: The command line field returned a bare dash, meaning Volatility could not recover the arguments from memory — this is itself a weak signal (arguments may have been paged out or the process exited before the snapshot). The orphaned parent (PPID 6956 absent from pslist) and the 9-second lifespan are consistent with a transient execution stage, but they are also consistent with a legitimate short-lived shell spawned by an already-exited parent (e.g. an installer or update process). Without a recoverable command line, DLL evidence, malfind hits, or network connections, there is insufficient evidence to confirm malice. The privilege set mirrors PID 2380 almost exactly (same broad token including SeTcbPrivilege and SeDebugPrivilege in the same non-enabled state), which may indicate both processes inherited the same elevated token from a common ancestor, but this cannot be confirmed from the available evidence alone. Marked INCONCLUSIVE pending recovery of the command line or correlation with PID 2380's batch file activity.

evidence.content: 2388	cmd.exe	7	SeTcbPrivilege		Act as part of the operating system

findings.justification: ctfmon.exe is a user-mode Collaborative Translation Framework monitor that normally runs under a standard interactive user token with a minimal privilege set. The token attached to PID 3712 contains virtually every Windows privilege (SeCreateTokenPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeDebugPrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, SeRestorePrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, and more), which is consistent with a SYSTEM-level token and wholly inconsistent with a legitimate ctfmon.exe instance. The orphaned parent (PPID 2620 absent from pslist) is a secondary concern — parent exit after spawning ctfmon is plausible — but the privilege footprint cannot be explained by normal operation. The three visible DLL entries are all clean System32 paths, and no malfind, netscan, or registry persistence slices were provided, so code injection and C2 activity cannot be assessed; this prevents a CONFIRMED verdict.

3712	ctfmon.exe	2	SeCreateTokenPrivilege		Create a token object

findings.justification: PID 5568 appears only in psscan (not in the live pslist/pstree), indicating it was already terminated and its EPROCESS block was recovered from pool memory. Its parent PID 560 is absent from the process list entirely, which is consistent with either a hollowed/exited parent or a process that was deliberately obscured. The process lived for approximately 10 seconds and directly spawned both PING.EXE (PID 5608, consistent with a connectivity check) and Client.exe (PID 5836, the next dropper stage), establishing this as the root of the attacker-controlled execution chain. The thread artefact (TID 2212, created at 19:26:50 — after the recorded ExitTime of 19:26:36) is a minor timeline anomaly likely attributable to thread-object lifetime outlasting the process, not fabrication, but it reinforces that this EPROCESS was recovered post-mortem.

Client.exe (PID 5836) is a non-system binary spawned by the orphaned cmd.exe (PID 5568). It lived for approximately 7 seconds and immediately spawned cmd.exe (PID 7980), which in turn launched the persistent Client.exe (PID 376) from AppData\Roaming\SubDir\. This recursive cmd→Client→cmd→Client pattern is a classic multi-hop dropper/launcher technique used to obscure the true origin of a persistent implant and to break simple parent-process attribution. The process is psscan-only (no live pslist entry), confirming it had already exited by the time of acquisition. No DLL or cmdline evidence was recovered for this PID, but the spawn chain alone — combined with the confirmed malicious terminus at PID 376 — is sufficient to confirm this as a staging artefact.

cmd.exe (PID 7980) was spawned by Client.exe (PID 5836) — an illegitimate parent for a command shell — and itself spawned both PING.EXE (PID 7552, likely another connectivity check) and the persistent Client.exe (PID 376) from C:\Users\kali\AppData\Roaming\SubDir\. The full path of the child is confirmed in pstree.txt, establishing that this transient shell was the direct installer/launcher of the persistent implant. The process exited approximately 10 seconds after creation, consistent with a one-shot launcher. Its role as the penultimate link in the dropper chain is unambiguous.

This is the confirmed persistent implant and the terminus of the entire dropper chain. Four independent signals converge: (1) the executable resides in C:\Users\kali\AppData\Roaming\SubDir\, a user-writable non-standard path with no legitimate Windows binary justification; (2) it has no ExitTime and holds 9 active threads, confirming it is still running at acquisition time; (3) its privilege token contains the near-complete Windows privilege set — including SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, SeCreateTokenPrivilege, and SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege — which is consistent only with a SYSTEM-level token or a stolen/duplicated token, not a normal user-context process launched from AppData; (4) MSCOREE.DLL is loaded, indicating a .NET binary, which is consistent with managed-code RAT/implant families. The combination of a user-writable path, a SYSTEM-equivalent token on a non-system binary, and its position as the sole surviving process in a multi-hop dropper chain makes this a high-confidence persistent implant.

376	Client.exe	10	SeLoadDriverPrivilege		Load and unload device drivers
376	Client.exe	29	SeImpersonatePrivilege		Impersonate a client after authentication
376	Client.exe	2	SeCreateTokenPrivilege		Create a token object

findings.justification: PID 5964 presents a convergence of anomalies that, taken together, are not consistent with any legitimate svchost instance. The process has zero threads (column 5 = 0) and no recorded command line — every legitimate svchost carries a -k <ServiceGroup> argument and hosts at least one thread. Despite this apparent emptiness, the token carries an unusually rich set of actively-enabled high-risk privileges: SeTcbPrivilege (Present,Enabled,Default), SeImpersonatePrivilege (Present,Enabled,Default), SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege (Present), SeBackupPrivilege (Present), SeRestorePrivilege (Present), and SeCreateGlobalPrivilege (Present,Enabled,Default) — a privilege set consistent with a SYSTEM-level token that has been injected or stolen rather than legitimately assigned to a service host. The process lived for exactly four minutes and then exited cleanly, which is consistent with a short-lived post-exploitation tool (e.g. a token-impersonation or lateral-movement helper) rather than a persistent service. The parent PID 896 is absent from the process list; while orphaned parents can be benign, combined with the other signals it removes the one anchor that could have legitimised this instance. The pstree entry does confirm the binary path as \Device\HarddiskVolume4\Windows\System32\svchost.exe and malware_pebmasquerade shows no PE header mismatch, so the binary on disk appears genuine — the most likely interpretation is process hollowing or token theft using the legitimate svchost image, or a short-lived injected thread that ran under a stolen SYSTEM token.

Three signals were raised by Agent 1; none individually or collectively reaches the threshold for CONFIRMED. The unexpanded '$(Arg0)' argument is the standard Task Scheduler token passed to taskhostw.exe for parameterised tasks and is not anomalous. The privilege set — while broad — is entirely consistent with a LOCAL SYSTEM token: the only privileges actually Enabled at the time of capture are SeChangeNotifyPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeCreateGlobalPrivilege, and SeDelegateSessionUserImpersonatePrivilege, all of which are default-enabled for SYSTEM-context processes; the remaining ~30 privileges are Present but not Enabled, which is the normal posture for a SYSTEM token before any privilege adjustment. The sole genuine anomaly is the orphaned parent (PPID 2168 absent from pslist), which is a real but weak signal: the Task Scheduler service host (svchost.exe) routinely exits or is recycled after spawning taskhostw, leaving the child orphaned in a snapshot. No corroborating evidence exists — DLLs load exclusively from C:\WINDOWS\system32\, no malfind sections were provided, no network connections, no registry persistence, and no suspicious handles — so the finding cannot be confirmed.

evidence.content: 6420	taskhostw.exe	29	SeImpersonatePrivilege	Present,Enabled,Default	Impersonate a client after authentication
